front: Film: disturbed Vietnam vet Travis Bickle becomes obsessed with saving underage prostitute Iris (Jodie Foster); plans to assassinate Senator Charles Palatine; features the improvised 'You talkin' to me?' mirror monologue

Film: Travis Bickle gives himself a Mohawk while planning to kill a senator — John Hinckley emulated this scene before shooting Reagan; director cameos as a man saying he'll kill his wife with a .44 magnum
Film: the director used a 'priest's-eye view' shot for a brothel massacre; Travis takes Betsy to see a Swedish sex education film; camera work influenced by Hitchcock's The Wrong Man
Film: Ray Liotta plays Henry Hill, who opens 'As far back as I can remember, I always wanted to be a gangster'; Joe Pesci plays Tommy DeVito, killed after being tricked into thinking he's made
Film: famous Copacabana long-take tracking shot follows Henry Hill and his girlfriend through the nightclub; one character is called 'Jimmy Two-Times' for repeating himself
Film: Robert De Niro plays boxer Jake LaMotta; shot in black and white; opening credits feature LaMotta jumping in slow motion to Mascagni's 'Cavalleria Rusticana: Intermezzo'
Film: ends with LaMotta reciting Terry Malloy's 'I coulda been a contender' monologue from Elia Kazan's On the Waterfront
Film: first collaboration between Robert De Niro, Harvey Keitel, and the director; Charlie covers for debt-ridden Johnny Boy and has a secret affair with his epileptic cousin Teresa
Film: ends with Cream's 'Steppin' Out' playing as Teresa's car crashes into a fire hydrant; made after John Cassavetes told the director his previous film Boxcar Bertha was 'a piece of shit'
Film: adapted from Hong Kong's Infernal Affairs; set in Boston; delivered the director's sole Best Director Academy Award win in 2007

Film: based on Charles Brandt's unverified book I Heard You Paint Houses; follows Frank Sheeran among the Philadelphia mob and Teamsters; de-aged actors with CGI without motion tracking markers; Al Pacino's first collaboration with the director
Film: stars Asa Butterfield as a French boy whose dead father tried to repair an automaton; the boy befriends Isabelle, daughter of film pioneer Georges Méliès
Film: 2023 Osage murders epic; Lily Gladstone translated scenes into Osage and helped restructure it to avoid a 'white-savior' narrative
back: Killers of the Flower Moon (Martin Scorsese)

front: Film: concert film of The Band's final show; sometimes called the greatest concert film ever made; a 'traveling booger matte' was invented to hide cocaine in Neil Young's nose
Director: his documentary about his parents Catherine and Charles at their Elizabeth Street apartment — which includes a meatball recipe in the credits — is his personal 'best film'
Director: his 1995 BBC documentary A Personal Journey classifies directors as storytellers, illusionists, smugglers, or iconoclasts
Director: founded the World Cinema Project to restore non-Western films, including Ousmane Sembene's Black Girl, Lino Brocka's Manila in the Claws of Light, and Iran's Chess Game of the Wind
Director: Paul Schrader wrote the screenplays for his films Taxi Driver and Raging Bull; Schrader's book The Transcendental Style in Film analyzes Dreyer, Bresson, and Ozu
Director: longtime editor Thelma Schoonmaker is tied with Michael Kahn for the most Academy Award nominations in the editing category
Director: had a cameo as Vincent van Gogh in Akira Kurosawa's 1990 anthology film Dreams
Director: trademark use of circular iris shots (foot in New York Stories, ring in The Age of Innocence, Matt Damon in The Departed); directed the pilot of HBO's Boardwalk Empire
